title: Build & Strategize — Run the Bases
intro:
STEP 5 OF 8 · MODULE 5

Build & Strategize — Run the Bases

Mission: Sequence the conversation so the customer experiences clarity, not overwhelm. Isolate → expand → protect → reinforce.

OBJECTIVE
SEQUENCE THE CALL
FRAMEWORK
BASEBALL DIAMOND
PRIMARY KPI
BASE ORDER %
FAILURE MODE
KITCHEN-SINKING

sections.title: The Baseball Diamond — Why Order Wins
sections.html:
The Baseball Diamond — Why Order Wins

The single most common reason a customer says “let me think about it” is not price — it’s overwhelm. When an advisor presents the safety concern, the maintenance items, the upsells, and the recovery item in one breath, the customer’s brain freezes. The Baseball Diamond is the discipline that prevents this: one base at a time, in order, with authorization at each base before advancing.

Figure 5.1 · The Base-Selling Diamond — start at 1st (right), touch every base in order, never skip
BaseTopicGoalToneAuthorization Required Before Advancing?
1st BaseThe customer’s original concern (or safety if no concern)Solve what they came forConfident, focusedYES
2nd BaseAdditional inspection findingsBundle with 1st-base authorizationAssumptive, calmYES
Short StopRe-open a missed safety sale (fresh voice)Save the unsafe carConcerned, not pushyYES (or schedule)
3rd BaseScheduled maintenance due now or within 10kLong-term ownership protectionSoft, no urgency stackingYES (or schedule)
Home PlateFinal invoice reviewReinforce value, set up next visitWarm, completeN/A (closing summary)

sections.title: The Two Frameworks Inside Every Base
sections.html:
The Two Frameworks Inside Every Base

FRAMEWORK A

Good · Good · Bad

Two positives prime the brain into a receptive state. The third statement (the cost or concern) then lands inside an open mind instead of a defensive one. Lead with bad news and you trigger fight-or-flight before the customer hears a single solution.

Example: “Good news: I have great DVI documentation. Good news: we identified the cause. The finding: the water pump is leaking.”

FRAMEWORK B

P · R · S

Problem · Risk · Solution. Every finding gets stated in this exact order, no skipping. The Risk step is the one most advisors omit — and it’s the one that converts information into urgency. Without Risk, the customer doesn’t know why to act now.

Follow PRS with the Total Investment, then the Coverage (warranty), then a tee-up sentence.

sections.title: The Anti-Kitchen-Sink Discipline
sections.html:
The Anti-Kitchen-Sink Discipline

STOPDo NOT mix bases on the same call.

Kitchen-sinking is the practice of dumping every finding (safety + maintenance + recovery + upsell) into one call. It feels efficient. It destroys trust. The customer hears a list of expenses and concludes “they’re trying to get me.” Each base is its own micro-conversation, with its own authorization, hung up between bases. Slower — and a meaningfully higher close rate.

sections.title: Mastery Check
sections.html:
Mastery Check

LEVEL 5 · BASE-SELLING MASTERY

  • Ran bases in correct order on every workflow (1st → 2nd → Short Stop if needed → 3rd → Home).
  • Used Good-Good-Bad framing on every base.
  • Stated full PRS on every finding — never skipped Risk.
  • Did not kitchen-sink. Hung up between bases. Re-called for the next base.
  • Tied total investment to coverage (warranty) without a pause.
COACH’S CORNERListen for the “everything” call.

Pull one recorded call per advisor per week. If the advisor presented more than one base in a single call, flag it. Replay it together. Have them re-script it as separate base calls. This single intervention has driven the most close-rate improvement of any coaching move in the playbook.

Base-Order Compliance
≥ 90%
% of workflows that ran the bases in proper order.
PRS Completeness
100%
% of findings that included all three: Problem, Risk, Solution.
Same-Call Mixing
≤ 5%
% of calls where the advisor presented more than one base.
